message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
This computer is a member of my tailscale network.  Help me set up a new linux computer on this tailscale at ip address 192.168.86.22

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Set up Linux computer on Tailscale network

content.text: I'll help you set up a new Linux machine on your tailnet. First, let me understand your current tailscale setup and check whether the new machine is reachable from here.

input.description: Ping the new machine on LAN

input.command: tailscale version 2>&1; echo "---"; tailscale ip -4 2>&1; echo "---EXIT:$?---"
input.description: Show tailscale version and this machine's tailscale IP

content.text:
This machine is `cerebro` (tailscale 1.98.4, Linux), and the new box at `192.168.86.22` is reachable on your LAN (0.2ms — same subnet). 

Now I need to get onto the new machine to install tailscale. Let me probe how it's accessible.

input.command: nc -z -w 2 192.168.86.22 22 2>&1 && echo "SSH port 22: OPEN" || echo "SSH port 22: closed/filtered"; echo "---"; ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o ConnectTimeout=3 -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new 192.168.86.22 'echo CONNECTED as $(whoami) on $(hostname); uname -a; cat /etc/os-release 2>/dev/null | grep -E "^(NAME|VERSION)="' 2>&1 | head -20; echo "---EXIT:${PIPESTATUS[0]}---"

SSH is open on `192.168.86.22`, but it defaulted to user `bhyslop` and my key isn't authorized yet (it wants a publickey or password). Everything else — installing tailscale and joining the tailnet — I can drive from here once I can log in.

I need a couple of things only you can provide:

options.description: You give me a username and add cerebro's public SSH key to that user's authorized_keys yourself, then I connect key-based.

options.description: It tried 'bhyslop' — tell me the actual username to use (with password or key).

options.description: I run 'tailscale up' and give you the login URL to open in a browser and approve. Simple, interactive.

options.label: Auth key
options.description: You generate a pre-auth key at login.tailscale.com/admin/settings/keys and paste it; I register the node non-interactively.

aiTitle: Set up Linux computer on Tailscale network

content.text: Good — browser login for the tailnet, and we'll set up passwordless SSH from here (`cerebro`) into the new box first. Let me check what SSH keys `cerebro` already has.

input.command: ls -la ~/.ssh/ 2>&1; echo "---PUBKEYS---"; for f in ~/.ssh/*.pub; do [ -e "$f" ] && echo "== $f ==" && cat "$f"; done 2>&1; echo "---EXIT:$?---"

-rw-------  1 bhyslop bhyslop  216 Mar 28 15:07 authorized_keys
-rw-------  1 bhyslop bhyslop  419 Mar 27 01:10 id_ed25519

-rw-------  1 bhyslop bhyslop 2604 Jun  2 11:43 known_hosts.old

---EXIT:0---

ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIEnlBvYX/1SrMcCNK9gaN/slGAV2NzdWtw7N3dwVZT9Y bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org
---EXIT:0---

content.text:
`cerebro` already has a perfectly good SSH keypair — `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` (created Mar 27). So there's nothing to create on this side; we just need to authorize this key on the new machine. That's a one-time step that needs the password, so **you** run it (I can't type into a password prompt).

Here's the whole path, so you see where we're headed:

1. **Authorize cerebro's key on the new box** — one-time password ← *you do this next*
2. **Verify passwordless SSH works** — I do this
3. **Install tailscale on the new box** — I do this
4. **`tailscale up`** → I hand you a browser URL to approve — you click, I confirm it joined

**Step 1 — run this in your prompt** (the `!` prefix runs it in this session so the password prompt works):

```
! ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub bhyslop@192.168.86.22
```

It'll warn about the host authenticity (type `yes`), then ask for `bhyslop`'s password on the new machine. Once it reports the key was added, tell me and I'll take it from there.

A couple of checks before you run it:
- Is the username actually **`bhyslop`** on the new machine? If it's something else, change it in the command.
- This assumes the new box already has an SSH **server** running (port 22 answered, so it does) and that password auth is enabled (it advertised `password`, so it is). Good to go.

To create an ed25519 keypair:

```
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-comment-here"
```

What it asks you:

1. **File location** — defaults to `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` (press Enter to accept). It writes two files:
   - `id_ed25519` — the **private** key (keep secret, never share)
   - `id_ed25519.pub` — the **public** key (safe to copy anywhere)
2. **Passphrase** — optional. Press Enter twice for no passphrase (passwordless, convenient for automation), or type one for extra protection at rest.

The `-C` comment is just a label (commonly `user@host` or your email) so you can tell keys apart later — it has no effect on security.

**A few useful flags:**
- `-f ~/.ssh/some_name` — write to a specific filename instead of the default (use this if you don't want to overwrite an existing `id_ed25519`)
- `-N ''` — set an empty passphrase non-interactively (no prompts at all)

So a fully non-interactive one-liner:
```
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -N '' -C "bhyslop@$(hostname)"
```

---

Note for our actual task: **`cerebro` already has `~/.ssh/id_ed25519`** — you don't need to create one here. You'd only run `ssh-keygen` if you want a *brand-new* key, or if you're creating a key *on the new machine* for it to reach other hosts later (a separate concern from logging *into* it).

Were you asking so you can generate a fresh key, or just curious about the mechanics? If you want to proceed with passwordless login to the new box, the next step is still the `ssh-copy-id` command from my previous message.

input.command: ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o ConnectTimeout=5 -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new bhyslop@192.168.86.22 'echo CONNECTED as $(whoami)@$(hostname); echo "---OS---"; cat /etc/os-release 2>/dev/null | grep -E "^(PRETTY_NAME|VERSION)="; echo "---ARCH---"; uname -m; echo "---SUDO---"; sudo -n true 2>/dev/null && echo "passwordless sudo: YES" || echo "passwordless sudo: NO (will need password)"; echo "---TS---"; command -v tailscale && tailscale version 2>/dev/null || echo "tailscale: NOT installed"' 2>&1; echo "---EXIT:$?---"
input.description: Test passwordless SSH and probe new machine

---ARCH---
x86_64
---SUDO---
passwordless sudo: YES
---TS---
tailscale: NOT installed
---EXIT:0---

content.text:
Passwordless SSH works. The new machine is **`relic`** — Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS, x86_64, passwordless sudo, no tailscale yet. 

Installing tailscale now (official install script):

input.command: ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o ConnectTimeout=10 bhyslop@192.168.86.22 'curl -fsSL https://tailscale.com/install.sh | sh' 2>&1 | tail -30; echo "---EXIT:${PIPESTATUS[0]}---"

content.content:
bash: line 1: curl: command not found
---EXIT:0---

toolUseResult.stdout:
bash: line 1: curl: command not found
---EXIT:0---

content.text: No `curl` on the minimal Ubuntu image. Let me install curl (and retry the tailscale install) in one go:

input.command: ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o ConnectTimeout=10 bhyslop@192.168.86.22 'sudo apt-get update -qq && sudo apt-get install -y -qq curl >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "curl installed: $(curl --version | head -1)" || echo "curl install FAILED"' 2>&1 | tail -20; echo "---EXIT:${PIPESTATUS[0]}---"

content.text: Tailscale **1.98.4** installed (same version as cerebro) and the daemon's enabled. Now I'll start `tailscale up` — it prints a login URL and waits. I'll launch it detached so it keeps waiting for your approval, then grab the URL:

input.command: ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o ConnectTimeout=10 bhyslop@192.168.86.22 'sudo systemctl start tailscaled 2>/dev/null; sudo rm -f /tmp/ts-up.log; sudo nohup tailscale up --hostname relic >/tmp/ts-up.log 2>&1 & sleep 6; echo "===URL LOG==="; sudo cat /tmp/ts-up.log' 2>&1; echo "---EXIT:$?---"
input.description: Start tailscale up and capture login URL

content.text:
**Open this URL in your browser and approve the machine:**

## 👉 https://login.tailscale.com/a/ba486470189ee

It'll join as hostname **`relic`** under your account. Once you've clicked through and approved it, tell me and I'll confirm it came online and grab its tailnet IP.

content: Goal: add the new Linux box (relic, 192.168.86.22) to your tailnet. Tailscale is installed and waiting for approval. Next: open https://login.tailscale.com/a/ba486470189ee in a browser and approve relic, then tell me. (disable recaps in /config)
